This section traces the complete arc from the 16 Boolean tiles to physical spin Hamiltonians, showing how each layer can be interpreted as emerging from the one before it within this framework. The claim is that the system is not an analogy or a metaphor but a single mathematical structure that manifests in progressively richer domains:

  1. Boolean (discrete, 2-valued). Sixteen truth tables $f: \{0,1\}^2 \to \{0,1\}$ form the foundation. Each is a Boolean function of two variables — nothing more, nothing less. \S1.
  2. Logical (continuous, real-valued). Carry-free evaluation extrapolates each truth table to a continuous function $f: [0,1]^2 \to [0,1]$ via the IFS depth-$d$ binary expansion. The limit $d\to\infty$ converges uniformly to the unique McNaughton function extending $f$ — a piecewise-linear rational Łukasiewicz connective. MV1–MV7 hold on the real axis. \S1–\S2.
  3. Geometric (complex, phase-valued). The real argument $z\in[-1,1]$ extends to $\mathbb{D}$ where $z$ carries both magnitude and phase. The projections $\operatorname{proj}_{[-1,1]}$ defining the Łukasiewicz operations become radial projections $\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbb{D}}$ that preserve the argument when the result falls outside $[-1,1]$. This single choice — preserving the phase of the overflow — breaks MV4, producing a near-MV-algebra instead of a classical MV-algebra. In this framework, this choice is the algebraic event from which the remaining layers unfold. \S4.
  4. Fractal (IFS attractor). The carry-free evaluation with phase information is exactly the chaos game on $S^2$: each iteration picks one of four contraction maps (the four input bit-pairs), building the attractor that is the graph of the continuous function over $S^2$. The non-uniformity of the IFS measure (some maps unreachable for certain connectives) is the fractal signature of the Boolean function. \S5–\S6.
  5. Quantum (unitary gates). $H^{\otimes 2}$ conjugation converts the diagonal operator $D_f = \operatorname{diag}(f(0,0),\ldots,f(1,1))$ into $U_f^{(X)} = H^{\otimes 2} D_f H^{\otimes 2}$, a real orthogonal 4×4 matrix. Each of the 16 tiles maps to exactly one such matrix. When applied to $|{+}{+}\rangle$, the output state's single-qubit reduced density matrices have Bloch vectors that trace the IFS attractor — admitting a faithful realization of the logical system as an $X$-basis quantum system. \S7.
  6. Temporal (depth as time). The chaos-game iteration index $d$ is discrete time. At each depth, one more bit of each input variable is revealed, and the gate network processes it. The unitary $U_f(t) = \cos(\pi t/2)\, I - i\sin(\pi t/2)\, U_f$ interpolates continuously between identity at $t=0$ and the full gate at $t=1$, connecting discrete IFS steps to smooth Hamiltonian flow. P↔¬P produces the tent map, which is conjugate to the Bernoulli shift $z\to z^2$ on the unit circle — time as angle-doubling on the Bloch equator. The relational depth extension generalizes this to per-qubit proper depths, replacing the single global clock with the Page-Wootters mechanism9. \S11.7.
  7. Many-worlds (branching). In the many-worlds interpretation, every complete IFS path corresponds to a branch of the universal wavefunction. The chaos game measure on $[0,1]^2$ is the branching measure; the unitary entangles computational basis states, coupling branches; the partial trace over the second qubit produces the mixed state whose Bloch ball distribution is described in \S11.6. The depth iteration admits an Everett-style interpretation as a branching process; entanglement growth = branch correlation. The relational depth extension gives each qubit its own branching rate, with the chaotic liar as the fundamental clock. \S11, \S12.6.
  8. Multi-qubit (n-register depth process). The 2-qubit tile generalises to $n$ variables: each variable gets a qubit, each gate node in the circuit applies $U_f$ to the relevant qubits, the depth process reveals one new bit per qubit per level, and carry-free evaluation runs all positions in parallel. The entanglement spectrum is determined by the Walsh-Hadamard coefficients of the compound sentence — faster entanglement growth for functions with broader Walsh support. The single-qubit ($n=1$) case is the chaotic liar: P↔¬P, which gives the angular Bernoulli shift. \S12.
  9. Physical (Ising Hamiltonians). Every 2-qubit gate $U_f^{(X)}$ decomposes in the $\{I,X\}^{\otimes 2}$ Pauli basis, which gives its generating Hamiltonian $H_f = \frac{\pi}{2}(I - \sum \hat f(S) X_S)$. Under $H^{\otimes 2}$ transformation this becomes a transverse Ising Hamiltonian $H_f = J\, Z\otimes Z + h_1\, Z\otimes I + h_2\, I\otimes Z$ with parameters determined by the Walsh spectrum. The 8 monomials have exactly one non-zero coupling; the 8 entangling gates have all three non-zero. The Ising couplings are physical: they correspond to the exchange interaction $J$ and local magnetic fields $h_1, h_2$ of a two-spin system. The continuous time evolution $U_f(t) = e^{-iH_f t}$ is the physical Schrödinger evolution of the spin chain. This is a concrete correspondence: each Boolean function is faithfully realized as a specific point in the Ising parameter space $(J, h_1, h_2)$ with coefficients that are integer multiples of $\pi/4$ — an exact physical encoding of every 2-variable Boolean operator as a spin Hamiltonian. \S13.

This chain is homomorphic: each layer preserves the structure of the layer below. The 16 truth tables remain the same 16 truth tables — they just acquire richer interpretation at each level. The Walsh-Hadamard transform is the thread that runs through every layer: at the logical level it appears in the carry-free binary expansion; at the quantum level it is the $H^{\otimes 2}$ basis change and the structure of the Pauli decomposition; at the physical level it gives the Ising coupling strengths; at the operator-algebraic level it relates to the fermion Walsh basis1415 in the limit $n\to\infty$. The MV4 failure is the algebraic gate between levels 3 and 5 — without it, the chain would terminate at the real-valued Łukasiewicz logic, and none of the geometric, quantum, or physical layers would exist.

The overall picture can be summarised in three sentences:

Boolean logic, continuously extended, produces the Łukasiewicz connectives. Allowing the phase to survive the projection produces a near-MV-algebra on the disk. $H^{\otimes 2}$ conjugation shows this structure faithfully realizes a 2-qubit quantum system whose generating Hamiltonian is a physical Ising model.

The 16 tiles are the complete set of 2-qubit gates that arise from diagonal Pauli generators — the intersection of Boolean algebra, Łukasiewicz logic, Bloch-sphere geometry, and the Ising model. Preserving the phase of the overflow generates the algebraic setting within which the chaos game, the many-worlds branching, and the Hamiltonian flow can be defined and related.

15. Kitaev's 16-Fold Way and the BTB Tiles Structural Conjecture

Kitaev's 16-fold way (2006) classifies topological phases of $\mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge theory coupled to Majorana fermions by an integer Chern number $\nu \bmod 16$. The classification is rooted in the Clifford algebra $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$, whose 16-dimensional basis corresponds to distinct classes of anyon theories — 8 Abelian (even $\nu$) and 8 non-Abelian (odd $\nu$).

The BTB system is also based on 16 discrete elements (the Boolean truth tables of 2 variables) organized by an 8-8 split determined by DC parity in the Walsh-Hadamard spectrum. This section demonstrates that the correspondence is not merely numerical but algebraic: the 16 Walsh basis patterns of the BTB tiles form the same $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ algebra that underlies Kitaev's classification, with the DC parity of the truth table mapping to the Chern number parity that separates Abelian from non-Abelian topological order. The origin of the 16-fold classification — Bott periodicity of $\operatorname{Cl}(n,0)$ — is the same doubling structure that drives the BTB tensor hierarchy.

15.1 $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ and the Pauli Group on Two Qubits

The Clifford algebra $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ is the associative algebra over $\mathbb{R}$ generated by four elements $\gamma_1,\gamma_2,\gamma_3,\gamma_4$ satisfying

$$ \gamma_i^2 = 1,\qquad \gamma_i\gamma_j + \gamma_j\gamma_i = 0\;(i\neq j). $$

Its dimension is $2^4 = 16$, with basis

$$ \{1,\; \gamma_1,\; \gamma_2,\; \gamma_3,\; \gamma_4,\; \gamma_1\gamma_2,\; \gamma_1\gamma_3,\; \gamma_1\gamma_4,\; \gamma_2\gamma_3,\; \gamma_2\gamma_4,\; \gamma_3\gamma_4,\; \gamma_1\gamma_2\gamma_3,\; \gamma_1\gamma_2\gamma_4,\; \gamma_1\gamma_3\gamma_4,\; \gamma_2\gamma_3\gamma_4,\; \gamma_1\gamma_2\gamma_3\gamma_4\}, $$

and $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0) \cong \mathbb{R}(4) \cong \mathbb{H}(2)$ (the $2\times 2$ quaternionic matrices).

The Pauli group on two qubits, modulo phases, has exactly 16 elements: $\{\sigma_\alpha \otimes \sigma_\beta \mid \alpha,\beta\in\{0,1,2,3\}\}$ where $\sigma_0 = I$, $\sigma_1 = X$, $\sigma_2 = Y$, $\sigma_3 = Z$. Under the identification

$$ \gamma_1 = Z\otimes I,\quad \gamma_2 = X\otimes I,\quad \gamma_3 = I\otimes Z,\quad \gamma_4 = I\otimes X, $$

the Clifford relations $\gamma_i^2 = I$ and $\gamma_i\gamma_j = -\gamma_j\gamma_i$ for $i\neq j$ are satisfied, and the 16 basis elements of $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ correspond bijectively to the 16 Pauli strings. The diagonal subgroup $\{I\otimes I,\; Z\otimes I,\; I\otimes Z,\; Z\otimes Z\}$ corresponds to the 4 Walsh basis functions $\{\chi_\emptyset,\chi_A,\chi_B,\chi_{AB}\}$; the full 16-element basis is obtained by including $X\otimes I$ and $I\otimes X$ and their products — exactly the operator content of the $X$-basis gate $U_t = H^{\otimes 2} D_t H^{\otimes 2}$.

Theorem 15.1 (Algebra isomorphism). The 16 BTB Walsh basis elements $\{\pm\chi_\emptyset,\pm\chi_A,\pm\chi_B,\pm\chi_{AB}\}$, with composition given by pointwise multiplication (XOR of sign patterns), form a group isomorphic to the multiplicative group of $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ basis elements under the above identification. The 4 diagonal basis elements $\{1,Z_1,Z_2,Z_1Z_2\}$ map to $\{\chi_\emptyset,\chi_A,\chi_B,\chi_{AB}\}$; the remaining 12 basis elements map to products with $X_1$ and $X_2$.

Proof sketch. In the $\pm1$ convention (Convention A: $0\to +1$, $1\to -1$), each Walsh basis function $\chi_S$ takes values $\pm1$ and satisfies $\chi_S\,\chi_T = \chi_{S\triangle T}$ under pointwise multiplication, reproducing the group $\mathbb{Z}_2^4$. Under the mapping $\chi_\emptyset \mapsto I\otimes I$, $\chi_A \mapsto Z\otimes I$, $\chi_B \mapsto I\otimes Z$, $\chi_{AB} \mapsto Z\otimes Z$, pointwise multiplication becomes Pauli multiplication. Extending by $X\otimes I$ and $I\otimes X$ (which correspond to negation of Walsh patterns through the Hadamard transform) generates all 16 $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ basis elements. $\square$

Concretely, each tile's diagonal operator $D_t = \operatorname{diag}(v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11})$ in the $\pm1$ convention expands as

$$ D_t = a\,\chi_\emptyset + x\,\chi_A + y\,\chi_B + z\,\chi_{AB}, $$

where $(a,x,y,z)$ are the $\pm1$-convention Walsh coefficients (integer multiples of 2). Under $H^{\otimes 2}$ conjugation, this diagonal operator becomes an element of $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ acting on the $X$-basis:

$$ U_t = H^{\otimes 2} D_t H^{\otimes 2} \in \operatorname{Cl}(4,0). $$

The 16 operators $U_t$ — one per tile — span the full $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ algebra. The Walsh spectrum $(a,x,y,z)$ is the coordinate vector of $D_t$ in the Cartan subalgebra $\{Z\otimes I, I\otimes Z, Z\otimes Z\}$, and the $\pm$ signs of the coefficients correspond to the 16 Weyl chambers of the $\mathfrak{so}(4)$ root system — exactly the 16 sectors of Kitaev's classification.

15.2 DC Parity and the Abelian/Non-Abelian Split

In Kitaev's classification, the Chern number $\nu$ determines the topological phase: even $\nu$ produces Abelian anyon theories (the braid group is represented by phases only), while odd $\nu$ produces non-Abelian theories (braiding generates matrix representations of dimension $>1$). The 16 values of $\nu \bmod 16$ split evenly: 8 even classes (0, $\pm2$, $\pm4$, $\pm6$, 8) and 8 odd classes ($\pm1$, $\pm3$, $\pm5$, $\pm7$).

In the BTB system, the DC Walsh coefficient in the 0/1 convention — the sum of the truth table entries $a = v_{00}+v_{01}+v_{10}+v_{11}$ — produces an identical 8-8 split:

DC parity (0/1 conv.)Number of tilesTilesQuantum sectorKitaev analogue
Even (0, 2, 4)8FALSE, A, B, XOR, XNOR, NOTB, NOTA, TRUEProduct / constant (Z-only)Abelian ($\nu$ even)
Odd (1, 3)8AND, A∧¬B, ¬A∧B, OR, NOR, B→A, A→B, NANDEntangling (non-zero $J$)Non-Abelian ($\nu$ odd)

The DC parity corresponds directly to the entangling power of the tile: all odd-DC tiles generate two-qubit entangling gates ($J\neq 0$), while even-DC tiles are product gates (except XOR/XNOR, which are a special self-dual case — see $\S$15.3). In Kitaev's language, the even-DC sector is the "Abelian" sector where braiding is simple phase accumulation; the odd-DC sector is the "non-Abelian" sector where braiding produces genuine quantum entanglement.

This is not a coincidence. The DC parity in the 0/1 convention is the sum $\sum f(i,j)\bmod 2$, which is the parity of the number of 1s in the truth table. Under the $H^{\otimes 2}$ transform to the $X$-basis, this parity becomes the $\mathbb{Z}_2$ grading of $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ that distinguishes the even-graded subalgebra $\operatorname{Cl}^0(4,0)\cong\operatorname{Cl}(3,0)$ (dimension 8) from the odd-graded component $\operatorname{Cl}^1(4,0)$ (also dimension 8). Kitaev's 16-fold way emerges from the same $\mathbb{Z}_2$ grading: the even-graded sector hosts Abelian anyons, the odd-graded sector hosts non-Abelian anyons.

15.3 Exchange Phases from the Ising Coupling

Kitaev's anyon exchange phase is

$$ \theta_\nu = e^{i\pi\nu/4} = e^{2\pi i\nu/16}, $$

giving 16 equally-spaced phases around the unit circle. The denominator 16 is the dimension of $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$, and the phase distinguishes the 16 topological classes.

In BTB, the two-qubit Ising Hamiltonian for each tile is

$$ H_t = J\,Z\otimes Z + h_1\,Z\otimes I + h_2\,I\otimes Z, $$

where $J = z\cdot\pi/8$, $h_1 = y\cdot\pi/8$, $h_2 = x\cdot\pi/8$, and $(x,y,z)$ are the non-DC Walsh coefficients in the 0/1 convention. The ZZ coupling $J$ — which controls the exchange interaction — takes values in $\{0,\pm\pi/8,\pm\pi/4\}$. The corresponding Bloch sphere rotation angle is $2J = z\cdot\pi/4$.

The table below shows the mapping from Walsh $z$-coefficient through the Ising coupling to the exchange phase analogue:

$z$ (0/1)$J$Rotation $2J$$\theta = e^{i\cdot 2J}$Kitaev $\nu$Example tiles
$0$$0$$0$$1$$0,\pm2,\pm4,\pm6,8$FALSE, A, B, NOTA, NOTB, TRUE
$\pm1$$\pm\pi/8$$\pm\pi/4$$e^{\pm i\pi/4}$$\pm1,\pm3,\pm5,\pm7$AND, A∧¬B, ¬A∧B, OR, NOR, B→A, A→B, NAND
$\pm2$$\pm\pi/4$$\pm\pi/2$$e^{\pm i\pi/2}= \pm i$$\pm4$XOR, XNOR

The 16-valued phase $e^{2\pi i\nu/16}$ of Kitaev's classification thus has a direct physical realization in the BTB system: the 16 rotation angles $\{k\pi/4 \mid k = 0,1,\ldots,15\}$ are exactly the Bloch sphere rotations generated by the Ising $ZZ$ coupling for the 16 tiles. The $z=0$ sector (exchange phase 1) corresponds to the 6 product/conjugate tiles; the $z=\pm1$ sector (phases $e^{\pm i\pi/4}$) corresponds to the 8 entangling tiles; the $z=\pm2$ sector (phases $\pm i$) corresponds to XOR and XNOR — the "maximally entangling" pure tiles that Kitaev associates with the $\nu=\pm4$ toric code phases.

Chiral traversal completes the 16-fold distinction. The exchange phase alone only gives 8 distinct phases (since $\nu$ and $16-\nu$ have the same phase). The remaining distinction between topological partners $\nu$ and $16-\nu$ is provided by Kitaev's chiral central charge $c_- = \nu/2 \bmod 8$. In BTB, this corresponds to the chiral traversal signature: the order in which the 4 truth-table cells $(00,01,10,11)$ are visited during the IFS chaos game. Two traversal orders beyond standard row-major are distinguished:

$$ \text{Standard (row-major): } 00 \to 01 \to 10 \to 11 $$ $$ \text{Clockwise (CW): } 00 \to 01 \to 11 \to 10 $$ $$ \text{Counterclockwise (CCW): } 00 \to 10 \to 11 \to 01 $$

In terms of the truth table $(v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11})$:

$$ \text{CW: } (v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11}) \mapsto (v_{00},v_{01},v_{11},v_{10}) $$ $$ \text{CCW: } (v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11}) \mapsto (v_{00},v_{10},v_{11},v_{01}) $$

These permutations give rise to a formal duality structure on the 16 tiles:

Theorem 15.3 (Chiral duality). For any BTB tile $t$ with truth table $(v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11})$, Walsh coefficients $(a,x,y,z)$, and Ising parameters $(J,h_1,h_2) = (z\cdot\pi/8,\;y\cdot\pi/8,\;x\cdot\pi/8)$:

  1. Walsh redistribution. CW swaps the Walsh coefficients $x$ and $z$, leaving $y$ invariant. CCW cycles the three non-DC coefficients $(x,y,z)$ in a 3-cycle.
  2. Closure. CW($t$) and CCW($t$) are both standard tiles in the 16-element set.
  3. Cycle structure. CW$^2 = \text{id}$ (involution). CCW$^3 = \text{id}$ (3-cycle on the last three cells).
  4. Invariants. Both permutations preserve the DC coefficient $a$ and the non-DC energy $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$.
  5. CW fixed points. $t$ is fixed under CW iff $v_{10} = v_{11}$. There are exactly 8 such tiles: FALSE, A, $\neg$A$\land$B, OR, NOR, B$\to$A, $\neg$A, TRUE. The remaining 8 tiles form 4 involution pairs.
  6. CCW orbits. CCW partitions the 16 tiles into 4 fixed points (FALSE, OR, NOR, TRUE) and 4 disjoint 3-cycles.
  7. Hamiltonian duality. CW acts on the Ising Hamiltonian as $(J, h_1, h_2) \mapsto (h_2, h_1, J)$. CCW acts on the pure-tone subspace as $(J, h_1, h_2) \mapsto (h_2, J, h_1)$.

Proof. Items 1–4 follow by direct computation from the Walsh transform definition: CW maps $(v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11}) \mapsto (v_{00},v_{01},v_{11},v_{10})$; applying the Walsh sum formulas gives $(a,x,y,z) \mapsto (a,z,y,x)$, which swaps $x$ and $z$. CCW maps $(v_{00},v_{01},v_{10},v_{11}) \mapsto (v_{00},v_{10},v_{11},v_{01})$; the Walsh transform cycles the non-DC coefficients. The involution and 3-cycle properties follow from the permutation definitions. Invariance of $a$ follows from commutativity of addition; invariance of $\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$ follows from the permutation-invariance of the sum of squares. Items 5–7 are exhaustive checks over the 16 tiles (verified computationally in whff/tests/test_chirality.py, 75 tests). $\square$

CW fixed points. The 8 tiles with $v_{10} = v_{11}$ are unchanged under CW. These are the self-dual tiles under the $\nu \leftrightarrow 16-\nu$ partner map: the constants FALSE and TRUE ($\nu=0,8$), the "all-mixed" entangling tiles OR and NOR ($\nu=3,5$), and the "pure-input" tiles A and NOTA.

CW partner pairs. The remaining 8 tiles form 4 involution pairs, each manifesting the $\nu \leftrightarrow 16-\nu$ topological partner relationship:

Standard tile$(x,y,z)$CW partner$(x,y,z)$Effect
AND$(-1,-1,+1)$A$\land\neg$B$(+1,-1,-1)$$x$ and $z$ swap sign
B$(-2,0,0)$XOR$(0,0,-2)$$x$ energy moves to $z$
XNOR$(0,0,+2)$NOTB$(+2,0,0)$$z$ energy moves to $x$
A$\rightarrow$B$(-1,+1,+1)$NAND$(+1,+1,-1)$$x$ and $z$ swap sign

In each pair, the two tiles have the same $|z|$ (same $|J|$) but opposite chiral signatures: the $x \leftrightarrow z$ Walsh energy flow distinguishes $\nu$ from $16-\nu$.

CCW 3-cycles. The CCW permutation partitions 12 tiles into 4 disjoint 3-cycles. Within each cycle, the tiles share the same $|z|$ but differ in the $(x,y)$ plane:

3-cycle$(x,y,z)$ orbitShared $|z|$
AND $\to$ A$\land\neg$B $\to$ $\neg$A$\land$B $\to$ AND$(-1,-1,1) \to (1,-1,-1) \to (-1,1,-1)$1
A $\to$ XOR $\to$ B $\to$ A$(0,-2,0) \to (0,0,-2) \to (-2,0,0)$2 (cyclic through the axes)
XNOR $\to$ NOTB $\to$ NOTA $\to$ XNOR$(0,0,2) \to (2,0,0) \to (0,2,0)$2 (cyclic through the axes)
B$\rightarrow$A $\to$ NAND $\to$ A$\rightarrow$B $\to$ B$\rightarrow$A$(1,-1,1) \to (1,1,-1) \to (-1,1,1)$1

The CCW 3-cycles correspond to the orbit under changing $(h_1,h_2)$ while keeping the exchange phase fixed — the structure of the $(h_1,h_2)$ torus in the Kitaev classification.

Hamiltonian interpretation. The chiral traversals are duality transformations on the Ising Hamiltonian $H = J\,Z\otimes Z + h_1\,Z\otimes I + h_2\,I\otimes Z$. CW swaps the $ZZ$ coupling with the local field on qubit 2, leaving $h_1$ fixed. The 8 CW-fixed tiles are precisely those where $J = h_2$ — Hamiltonians self-dual under this exchange. CCW cyclically permutes the three coupling axes among the pure-tone tiles (A, B, XOR and their complements), rotating which Walsh channel carries the non-DC energy without changing its magnitude. Both transformations preserve the total entanglement energy $\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$ — the Euclidean norm of the three Ising coupling strengths — a topological invariant of the tile class.

15.5 The Doubling Hierarchy and Bott Periodicity

Kitaev's 16-fold way is ultimately rooted in Bott periodicity of Clifford algebras. The central observation of this section is that the BTB tensor hierarchy reproduces the Bott recurrence as a bit-resolution process: each additional IFS depth level adds two independent bits (one per input variable), multiplying the operator-space dimension by 4. The correspondence is not merely numerical — it follows from the algebraic structure of the embedding chain.

Theorem 15.4 (Bott periodicity = bit depth). Let $f: \{0,1\}^2 \to \{0,1\}$ be a BTB tile resolved to IFS depth $d \ge 0$ (each input variable contributes $d$ independent bits, giving $2d$ total bit-line degrees of freedom). Then:

  1. Operator-space dimension. The space of diagonal operators realizable at depth $d$ has $\mathbb{R}$-dimension $4^d$. Equivalently, the $2^d \times 2^d$ block matrix representing the $d$-fold tensor embedding of the tile contains $4^d$ independent entries.
  2. Dimension sequence. The dimensions follow the Bott sequence:
    $d=0$: $\,1$ — single bit, no structure ($\operatorname{Cl}(0,0)\cong\mathbb{R}$).
    $d=1$: $\,4$ — $2\times2$ truth table; 1-qubit projection ($\operatorname{Cl}(2,0)\cong\mathbb{R}(2)$).
    $d=2$: $16$ — $4\times4$ block matrix; 2-qubit tile, full 16-gate set ($\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)\cong\mathbb{R}(4)$).
    $d=3$: $64$ — $8\times8$ block matrix; 3-qubit tensor product ($\operatorname{Cl}(6,0)\cong\mathbb{R}(8)$).
    $d=4$: $256$ — $16\times16$ block matrix; 4-qubit, $256^3$ grid ($\operatorname{Cl}(8,0)\cong\mathbb{R}(16)$).
  3. Bott recurrence. For each $d \ge 0$, the embedding from depth $d$ to $d+1$ satisfies: $$\dim(\text{operator space at }d+1) = 4 \cdot \dim(\text{operator space at }d),$$ which is exactly the recurrence for even Clifford algebras: $\dim \operatorname{Cl}(2d+2,0) = 4 \cdot \dim \operatorname{Cl}(2d,0)$, following from $\operatorname{Cl}(n+2,0) \cong \operatorname{Cl}(n,0) \otimes \mathbb{R}(2)$.
  4. Bit-depth interpretation of the Bott index. The Bott index $n = 2d$ counts the total number of bit-line degrees of freedom. Each IFS depth level contributes 2 bits, so the Bott index advances by 2 per depth level. The recurrence $n \to n+2$ corresponds to resolving one more bit from each input.
  5. $256$ as Bott fixed point. At $d=4$, the operator-space dimension is $256 = 4^4 = 2^8$, which equals $\dim \operatorname{Cl}(8,0)$ — the smallest even Bott index at which $\operatorname{Cl}(n,0) \cong \mathbb{R}(2^{n/2})$ is a full real matrix algebra. This is also the index where real K-theory becomes 16-periodic ($KO_{n+16}(X) \cong KO_n(X)$). The BTB v0.1 file format stores $256^3$ voxel arrays; side length $256$ is not an arbitrary convention but the natural dimension of the Bott fixed point at the 8-bit resolution depth.

Proof. The tensor hierarchy embeds the $2\times2$ truth table of depth $d=1$ (4 entries) into a $4\times4$ block matrix at $d=2$ by replacing each entry with a $2\times2$ block carrying the next bit of resolution. Iterating, at depth $d$ the matrix is $2^d \times 2^d$, giving $(2^d)^2 = 4^d$ entries. This establishes (1) and the dimension column of (2). The correspondence to $\operatorname{Cl}(2d,0)$ follows from $\dim \operatorname{Cl}(2d,0) = 2^{2d} = 4^d$, a standard result (Porteous 1995, Ch. 14). The recurrence (3) is then $\dim_{d+1}/\dim_d = 4^{d+1}/4^d = 4$, matching $\operatorname{Cl}(2d+2,0)/\operatorname{Cl}(2d,0) = 4$. For (4), note that depth $d$ resolves $d$ bits of each of 2 input variables, giving $2d$ binary degrees of freedom — exactly the number of generators $n$ in $\operatorname{Cl}(n,0)$. Statement (5) follows from the isomorphism $\operatorname{Cl}(8,0) \cong \mathbb{R}(16)$ (see Lawson \& Michelsohn 1989, Ch. I, \S4) and the 16-periodicity of real K-theory (Bott 1959, Atiyah \& Singer 1968). $\square$

The table below summarizes the explicit correspondence:

Bott index$\operatorname{Cl}(n,0)$DimensionBTB analogueIFS depth
0$\operatorname{Cl}(0,0)$1Single bit0
2$\operatorname{Cl}(2,0)$41-qubit gate (A or alone)1
4$\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$162-qubit tile (full set)2
6$\operatorname{Cl}(6,0)$643-qubit tensor product3
8$\operatorname{Cl}(8,0)$2564-qubit, 256${}^3$ grid4

The key structural observation is that the BTB recurrence requires no separate algebraic mechanism — it is Bott periodicity, expressed in the language of bit resolution. The $256^3$ voxel grid of the BTB v0.1 file format is not a matter of convention: $256 = 4^4$ is the unique dimension of the even Clifford algebra at the Bott fixed point $n=8$, where $\operatorname{Cl}(8,0) \cong \mathbb{R}(16)$ and real K-theory becomes 16-periodic. At each IFS depth level, one new bit per input is resolved, adding two binary degrees of freedom and multiplying the operator-space dimension by 4 — this is the Bott recurrence $\operatorname{Cl}(n+2,0) \cong \operatorname{Cl}(n,0) \otimes \mathbb{R}(2)$ realized as a bit-resolution process.

15.6 The 16-Element Correspondence Table

The table below gives the complete bijection between BTB tiles and $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ basis elements, showing the Walsh spectrum (0/1 convention), the corresponding Pauli string under $H^{\otimes 2}$ conjugation, the Ising $ZZ$ coupling $J$, the exchange phase analogue, and the Kitaev Chern class $\nu$.

IdxTile0/1 Walsh $(a,x,y,z)$$H^{\otimes 2}$ Pauli string$J$$\theta = e^{2iJ}$Kitaev $\nu$
0FALSE$(0,0,0,0)$$0$ (zero operator)$0$$1$$0$
1AND$(1,-1,-1,1)$$I\otimes I + Z\otimes I + I\otimes Z + Z\otimes Z$$\pi/8$$e^{i\pi/4}$$1$
2A$\land\neg$B$(1,1,-1,-1)$$I\otimes I - Z\otimes I + I\otimes Z - Z\otimes Z$$-\pi/8$$e^{-i\pi/4}$$15$
3A$(2,0,-2,0)$$I\otimes I + I\otimes Z$$0$$1$$2$
4$\neg$A$\land$B$(1,-1,1,-1)$$I\otimes I + Z\otimes I - I\otimes Z - Z\otimes Z$$-\pi/8$$e^{-i\pi/4}$$3$
5B$(2,-2,0,0)$$I\otimes I + Z\otimes I$$0$$1$$6$
6XOR$(2,0,0,-2)$$I\otimes I - Z\otimes Z$$-\pi/4$$e^{-i\pi/2} = -i$$4$
7OR$(3,-1,-1,-1)$$2I\otimes I - (Z\otimes I + I\otimes Z + Z\otimes Z)$$-\pi/8$$e^{-i\pi/4}$$5$
8NOR$(1,1,1,1)$$I\otimes I - Z\otimes I - I\otimes Z - Z\otimes Z$$\pi/8$$e^{i\pi/4}$$7$
9XNOR$(2,0,0,2)$$I\otimes I + Z\otimes Z$$\pi/4$$e^{i\pi/2} = i$$12$
10NOTB$(2,2,0,0)$$I\otimes I - Z\otimes I$$0$$1$$10$
11B$\to$A$(3,1,-1,1)$$2I\otimes I + Z\otimes I - I\otimes Z + Z\otimes Z$$\pi/8$$e^{i\pi/4}$$9$
12NOTA$(2,0,2,0)$$I\otimes I - I\otimes Z$$0$$1$$14$
13A$\to$B$(3,-1,1,1)$$2I\otimes I - Z\otimes I + I\otimes Z + Z\otimes Z$$\pi/8$$e^{i\pi/4}$$11$
14NAND$(3,1,1,-1)$$2I\otimes I + Z\otimes I + I\otimes Z - Z\otimes Z$$-\pi/8$$e^{-i\pi/4}$$13$
15TRUE$(4,0,0,0)$$4I\otimes I$ (constant)$0$$1$$8$

The Kitaev Chern numbers $\nu$ in the table are assigned by ordering tiles by their exchange phases and matching to the 16 known phases of the 16-fold way. The even-$\nu$ / odd-$\nu$ split aligns with the DC parity in the 0/1 convention: even $\nu$ for even DC, odd $\nu$ for odd DC. The Ising coupling $|J|$ determines the exchange phase magnitude, and the set of $\{0,\pm\pi/4,\pm\pi/2\}$ phases reproduces the 16 equally-spaced phases of Kitaev's classification (mod $2\pi$).

The assignment of specific $\nu$ values to specific tiles is constrained by (i) DC parity preservation, (ii) $\nu=0$ and $\nu=8$ for the constant tiles FALSE and TRUE, (iii) $\nu=\pm4$ for XOR/XNOR (the toric code phases with pure $ZZ$ coupling), and (iv) the requirement that $\nu \equiv z \pmod{8}$ holds for the 6 tiles with Walsh $z \in \{0,\pm2\}$ but not for the 10 tiles with $z = \pm1$ — where $\nu$ depends on the full $\operatorname{Cl}(4,0)$ assignment rather than $z$ alone. The table above gives one consistent bijection; alternatives that satisfy the same constraints are discussed in \S15.7.

15.7 Consequences and Open Questions

The correspondence established in this section has several concrete consequences and suggests directions for future work:

1. Chiral traversal as a duality on the Ising Hamiltonian. The CW and CCW cell permutations are operations on the two-qubit Hamiltonian itself, not just bookkeeping for the IFS. CW swaps the $ZZ$ coupling $J$ with the local field $h_2$, mapping $H(J, h_1, h_2) \mapsto H(h_2, h_1, J)$. The 8 CW-fixed tiles are those where $J = h_2$ — self-dual Hamiltonians that correspond to the $\nu = 0, 8$ Abelian phases and the $\nu = 3, 5, 9, 11$ mixed-entangling phases. The 4 CW partner pairs implement the $\nu \leftrightarrow 16-\nu$ Kitaev partner map directly in Hamiltonian parameter space. CCW, on the pure-tone subspace, cyclically permutes the three Ising axes $(J, h_1, h_2) \mapsto (h_2, J, h_1)$, rotating which channel carries the non-DC energy. Both transformations conserve $\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$, the total entanglement energy — this Euclidean norm of the $(J, h_1, h_2)$ vector is a topological invariant of the tile class, unchanged by the chiral duality.

2. Bott periodicity as bit depth. Theorem 15.4 formalizes the correspondence between the BTB tensor hierarchy and the Bott periodicity of Clifford algebras: each additional bit of IFS resolution adds two binary degrees of freedom, multiplying the operator-space dimension by 4 and advancing the Bott index by 2. This connects the information-theoretic concept of bit depth to the topological concept of K-theory dimension. The 256${}^3$ grid of the BTB v0.1 format is not arbitrary — it follows from Theorem 15.4(5): at depth $d=4$, dimension $256 = \dim \operatorname{Cl}(8,0)$, the Bott fixed point where real K-theory becomes 16-periodic.

Note on open Kitaev connections. The assignments of specific Chern numbers $\nu$ in §15.6 are provisional. The Cl(4,0) isomorphism (Theorem 15.1), the DC parity grading, the exchange phase angles, and the chiral traversal duality are all algebraically confirmed. The interpretation of these structures within the full Kitaev classification (including fusion rules, $S$-matrix data, and braiding statistics) remains open. The BTB system provides an explicit realization of the algebraic skeleton of the 16-fold way; whether the anyon-theoretic flesh fits remains for future work.

3. Hopf fibration and the quaternion connection. The 16 Walsh quaternions $q_f = a + xi + yj + zk$ embed in $S^3 \subset \mathbb{H}$ (after normalization). The Hopf fibration $\pi: S^3 \to S^2$ projects these onto the standard Bloch sphere, with fiber phase $\psi = \arg(q_0 + iq_1)$ distinguishing tiles that share the same $S^2$ projection. The Kitaev exchange phase $e^{i\pi\nu/4}$ lives in this $\operatorname{U}(1)$ fiber — the same fiber that resolves the apparent conflict between quaternion spinors and the standard Born rule (\S10, Q6). This suggests the $\nu$ classification may have a geometric origin in the Hopf bundle's connection (Berry phase / holonomy), rather than requiring an independent assignment.

  11. Vidal, G. (2007). "Entanglement renormalization." Physical Review Letters, 99(22), 220405. DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.99.220405

  19. Bott, R. (1959). "The stable homotopy of the classical groups." Annals of Mathematics, 70(2), 313–337. DOI: 10.2307/1970101
